content: I'm wondering if there is an easy way to run all the test cases in .github/workflows/ci.yaml locally? How do people test their code?

I let GitHub run them in my fork. If you want to run them locally, you can self-host a runner: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/hosting-your-own-runners/about-self-hosted-runners but I am not sure how complex setting it up / maintaining it is.

https://github.com/nektos/act is a thing for running actions locally, I've never used it.

I think maybe we should put the tests into a .sh script or something that we can just run locally :-)
I guess for my small use case it would be much more economical to write a small script with excerpts from ci.yaml. It is much easier than maintaining a local runner.
We may switch to a script, or use TuxSuite, or perhaps the improved test support improves to the point it is good enough, etc.
Yeah, @Alex Gaynor @Wei Liu I think putting the (non-setup) steps in a script is definitely reasonable.

Alex Gaynor said:

https://github.com/nektos/act is a thing for running actions locally, I've never used it.

I think maybe we should put the tests into a .sh script or something that we can just run locally :-)

In one of the projects I work on the test environment is packaged as a docker container, so the CI and developers can run the exact same environment. It looks non-trivial to convert Rust for Linux to that model though.
In our case, while it is convenient to have everything exactly the same, it also means everyone would only ever test within that environment in order to pass the CI... :P
You're saying that as if software engineers don't already do it in their daily work. I believe placating the CI is a big part of a lot of engineers' work life. :-)
Of course, but in the kernel we don't have a single "production environment" to target, thus having the CI green does not mean as much.

At the moment, the CI is more like a smoke test, and still mostly focused on kbuild/arch/toolchain issues.

It also doubled for a while as a "nightly" to catch issues on e.g. new compilers. I have to give TuxSuite a go and perhaps setup there the actual nightlies to test rustc nightlies etc.

But, yeah, we can definitely improve it, and there are many things not covered (e.g. doing allmodconfig builds). It is unclear also after mainline how we will do things. Perhaps -next can do some of it for us...
